name: Ne Zha Full
description: Ne Zha, also known as the Third Lotus Prince, is a powerful deity in Chinese mythology. Born during the Shang Dynasty, his birth was far from ordinary. His mother, Lady Yin, was pregnant for three years and six months before giving birth to a lotus flower from which Ne Zha emerged as a young boy. He possessed immense strength and supernatural abilities from birth. Ne Zha's rebellious spirit and strong sense of justice often led him into conflict, most notably with Ao Guang, the Dragon King of the Eastern Sea. After a series of incidents, including killing Ao Guang's son, Ne Zha dismembered his own body to appease the Dragon King and protect his family and village. However, his master, Taiyi Zhenren, resurrected him using lotus roots and flowers, granting him even greater power and new weapons. Ne Zha became a formidable warrior, assisting King Wu of Zhou in overthrowing the Shang Dynasty. He is often depicted as a youthful warrior riding on wind fire wheels, wielding a spear and a Qiankun Circle (Universe Ring), and is revered as a protector deity, a symbol of courage, and a champion of the people. He represents filial piety, even in his act of self-sacrifice, and his rebirth signifies resilience and the triumph of good over evil. Ne Zha's story is a cornerstone of Chinese folklore and continues to inspire and entertain audiences through various forms of media.
subtitle: The Third Lotus Prince, Rebel God
achievements: Aiding King Wu in Overthrowing the Shang Dynasty
Self-Sacrifice for the Sake of His Family
Mastery of the Wind Fire Wheels

keyMyths.title: Birth from a Lotus
keyMyths.description: Ne Zha was born from a lotus flower after his mother's prolonged pregnancy. This miraculous birth signified his divine nature and extraordinary destiny.

keyMyths.title: Conflict with Ao Guang
keyMyths.description: Ne Zha's clashes with Ao Guang, the Dragon King of the Eastern Sea, led to significant events, including the death of Ao Guang's son and Ne Zha's subsequent self-sacrifice.

keyMyths.title: Resurrection and Empowerment
keyMyths.description: Taiyi Zhenren resurrected Ne Zha using lotus roots and flowers, granting him new life and enhanced abilities, enabling him to become a powerful warrior.

sources.author: Xu Zhonglin
sources.description: A major source for Ne Zha's mythology, detailing his birth, conflicts, and eventual role in the overthrow of the Shang Dynasty.
sources.text: A major source for Ne Zha's mythology, detailing his birth, conflicts, and eventual role in the overthrow of the Shang Dynasty.

sources.author: Various
sources.description: Ne Zha's stories are passed down through oral traditions and various forms of Chinese folklore.
